title: Student Empowerment Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
plain_summary: The Student Empowerment Act changes the rules for 529 education savings accounts so families can use the money for kindergarten through high school expenses, not just college costs. Parents can now use these tax-free savings to pay for things like school books, tutoring, test fees, and special help for students with disabilities. The money can be used whether kids go to public schools, private schools, religious schools, or are taught at home. This gives families more choices about how to spend their education savings to help their children learn.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Student Empowerment Act.

sections.title: Section 2  529 Account Funding for Homeschool and Additional Elementary and Secondary Expenses
sections.text: This section changes the tax rules about 529 education savings accounts. These accounts are special savings plans that help families save money for education costs without paying extra taxes. Right now, families can use this money for college expenses. This law would also let families use the money for elementary and secondary school costs, which means kindergarten through twelfth grade. The money can be used for students who go to public schools, private schools, religious schools, or who are taught at home. Families can pay for many different school-related costs with this money. They can pay for tuition, which is the fee charged by schools. They can buy curriculum materials and books that students need for their classes. They can pay for online learning materials and educational websites. Families can use the money for tutoring or special classes outside the home, but only if the teacher is not related to the student and meets certain requirements. The teacher must either have a teaching license from any state, have taught at a college or university, or be an expert in the subject they are teaching. The money can pay for test fees, including standardized tests that compare students across the country, advanced placement tests that can earn college credit, and tests needed to apply to colleges. Families can use the money for dual enrollment programs where high school students take college classes at the same time. The money can also pay for special educational therapies for students with disabilities, such as occupational therapy, behavioral therapy, physical therapy, and speech therapy. These services must be provided by someone who is licensed or certified to do this work. All of these expenses are allowed whether the student goes to a regular school or is homeschooled. This rule applies even if state law treats the homeschool as a private school instead of a homeschool. These changes will start applying to money taken out of 529 accounts after this law is signed.
